coredata.dc:title: Gold conjugated carbon dots nano assembly: FRET paired fluorescence probe for cysteine recognition
coredata.dc:creator: Sidhu, Jagpreet Singh
Singh, Ashutosh
Garg, Neha
Kaur, Navneet
Singh, Narinder
coredata.dc:description: The detection and discrimination of Cys amino acid from numerous other related biomolecules has great importance in clinical field for diagnosis of various diseases. Herein, to detect the Cys, we embedded the carbon dots (CDs), gold, and naphthalimide (L1) into a single ratiometric fluorescence sensor assembly. Sensor assembly works on the principle of FRET mechanism between CDs and naphthalimide when CDs and L1 adhered on gold nanoparticles surface. Gold metal was turned into solid support by in situ reduction of HAuCl4 in the presence of CDs and L1. When the assembly was excited at 360 nm, emission maxima at 568 nm corresponded to naphthalimide emission was emerged that signifies the existence of a FRET between the CDs and naphthalimide fluorophores. With the addition of Cys, the FRET mechanism eliminated and the change in the fluorescence emission at two different wavelengths (450 nm and 568 nm) was recorded. The endogenous images of Cys was recorded by collecting the fluorescence images of HeLa cells under fluorescence microscope and also applied for the assay of Cys in blood serum. Cytotoxicity studies of CDs and sensor assembly were evaluated by performing the MTT assay.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of sensor assembly
Study of FRET pair and cys sensing mechanism
Application of sensor complex for blood serum and cellular imaging
Intracellular imaging of cys in HeLa cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of L1 and Sensor Assembly.

Characterization of CDs (A) Powder XRD pattern of CDs demonstrate the disorder of carbon atom on CDs surface. (B) FTIR spectrum of CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs exhibiting two absorption band at 335 and 248 nm. (D) Emission spectra of CDs indicate the excitation-dependent emission.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (black line) and sensor assembly (blue line). (B) TEM image of sensor assembly showing the irregular adsorption of CDs/Naph on gold nanoparticles surface. Prepared sensor have not well-defined shape and showing the clusters of particles (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of sensor assembly upon gradual addition of Cys from 0 to 200 nM (D) Linear relationship of F450/F568 upon addition of Cys (E) F450/F568 response of sensor assembly in the presence of interfering biomolecules (100 μM each) (F) Graphical representation of sensing mechanism of Cys (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Fluorescence Images of HeLa cells (A) HeLa cells after 1 h of incubation with sensor assembly (B) HeLa cells after 2 h of incubation with sensor assembly (C) Fluorescence Images of Sensor assembly treated cells. These cells were Pre-incubated with NMM to quench all the endogenous sulfides for 1 h.
Fluorescence decay time and quantum yield.
Determination of Cys in blood serum.

head.ce:title: Gold conjugated carbon dots nano assembly: FRET paired fluorescence probe for cysteine recognition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Ropar, Rupnagar, Punjab 140001, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Kamand, Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, 175005, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Panjab University Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots and naphthalimide derivative molds into sensor assembly and addition of cysteine into sensor solution eliminates the FRET mechanism.

The detection and discrimination of Cys amino acid from numerous other related biomolecules has great importance in clinical field for diagnosis of various diseases. Herein, to detect the Cys, we embedded the carbon dots (CDs), gold, and naphthalimide (L1) into a single ratiometric fluorescence sensor assembly. Sensor assembly works on the principle of FRET mechanism between CDs and naphthalimide when CDs and L1 adhered on gold nanoparticles surface. Gold metal was turned into solid support by in situ reduction of HAuCl4 in the presence of CDs and L1. When the assembly was excited at 360 nm, emission maxima at 568 nm corresponded to naphthalimide emission was emerged that signifies the existence of a FRET between the CDs and naphthalimide fluorophores. With the addition of Cys, the FRET mechanism eliminated and the change in the fluorescence emission at two different wavelengths (450 nm and 568 nm) was recorded. The endogenous images of Cys was recorded by collecting the fluorescence images of HeLa cells under fluorescence microscope and also applied for the assay of Cys in blood serum. Cytotoxicity studies of CDs and sensor assembly were evaluated by performing the MTT assay.

ce:section.ce:para: Amongst the series of amino acids, cysteine (Cys) is the prime essential amino acid which is involved in the synthesis of various proteins and metabolic reactions of living beings [1–3]. However, deficiency of Cys is associated with assorted diseases such as lethargy, weakness, liver damage, and skin problems [4–6]. Therefore, accurate and reliable monitoring of Cys in living systems has preeminent importance for diagnosis as well as treatment of disorders at an early stage. Although, several analytical tools such as HPLC, electrophoresis and some others have been established for its detection, however, these are much complicated and involve expensive procedures to operate [7–9]. Therefore, simple and economical sensor techniques are still required to the extreme demand for analysis of biological samples without interference from other related biomolecules. In last few years, fluorescence sensors gained a lot of consideration for the analysis and monitoring of various biological important analytes owing to their high selectivity, simplicity of implementation, and effective for real-time analysis of intracellular biomolecules at subcellular levels [10,11]. However, significant efforts have been devoted in the last decades to expand the novel Cys selective fluorescent probes [12–15]. Recently, Wu et. al. developed a BODIPY based fluorescence sensor for the recognition of Cys and Hcy over other biothiols. The dye was decorated with formyl group which reacted with Cys/Hcy molecules and produced the change in fluorescence emission signal at single wavelength [16]. Deng et. al. developed a metal displacement approach using CDs and gold nanoparticles assembly developed to discriminate Cys from Hcy and GSH. They assembled CDs shells on gold nanoparticles and demonstrated the desorption of CDs from gold nanoparticles surface due to high affinity of –SH group towards gold particles [17]. However, these probes gives a response to such analytes only at a single point of wavelength [18]. The change in fluorescence intensity at a single emission wavelength in response to analytes is easily influenced through external factor such as temperature, concentration, viscosity, and excitation wavelength [13,19–21]. Therefore, more precise and accurate fluorescence probes are required to avoid the drawbacks of single point emission window. In this context, the ratiometric fluorescent receptors are the most effective alternative approach than single switch ‘ON/OFF’ sensors. Ratiometric sensor considers the fluorescence emission ratio at two different wavelengths which outweigh the effect of external factors. For the development of such ratiometric sensor, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a suitable methodology which works on the principle of the transfer of energy from energy donor unit to energy acceptor moiety [22–25]. FRET sensor offers high accuracy and provides more superior results over the single wavelength emission probe by eliminating the interference of background signals [26–28]. Although, some organic fluorophore based ratiometric fluorescence sensor has been developed in literature reports. However, they are not much selective towards Cys molecule. Ma et.al. also developed a BODIPY based ratiometric fluorescence sensor, however, this sensor was not enabled to discriminate between Cys and Hcys molecule. Therefore, the development of highly selective and specific probe for Cys detection has great demand [7]. Moreover, nanohybrid technology containing organic and inorganic probe has recently emerged a new field of sensing technology. Thus, herein, to synthesize the sensor based on nanohybrid technology, we chose carbon dots (CDs) and 3-aminonaphthalimide (L1) as an energy donor and an energy acceptor entity respectively. CDs are the zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon material having a size less than 10 nm [29–31]. Due to their distinctive photophysical and electronic properties, CDs gained the tremendous attention of the scientific community to build fluorescent sensors for biological applications [32–36]. CDs hold pronounced promises in the sense of their easy synthesis procedure, negligible cytotoxicity, high water solubility, high photostability, and easy excretion through nephrons over the organic dyes [37–40]. Furthermore, along with the ratiometric approach, brightness and photostability of probe could also be improved by using CDs [41]. Therefore, CDs are auspicious luminescent material for the sensing of biological analytes and cellular imaging [42,43] [44]. These conjugates have also been employed in the endogenous monitoring of Cys, however, they exhibited the fluorescence “ON-OFF” behavior only at a single wavelength. [17,45]. In fact, designing and synthesis of ratiometric fluorescence sensor, which gives change in the fluorescence emission at two different wavelengths still have been a challenge to solve the problems. Therefore, herein, to encounter this problem ratiometric ‘sensor assembly’ for the detection of Cys has been designed and synthesized. Gold nanoparticles were in situ prepared which act as solid support on which two fluorophore units: CDs and L1 adhered. When this sensor assembly was excited at λex = 360 nm, the fluorescence emission of L1 unit was observed at 568 nm, however, the addition of Cys leads to the elimination of energy transfer and emission of CDs started to recover. Furthermore, with excellent biocompatibility and selectivity of the prepared sensor as predicted from MTT assay, cellular images of endogenous Cys in HeLa cells were recorded. As compared to reported ratiometeric fluorescent probes, this nano assembly consists of two fluorophores connected through gold particles support. Combination of organic ligand and carbon dots through gold linkage to get ratiometric change in fluorescence signal is the novel approach till date for detection of cysteine molecule.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of sensor assembly
ce:section.ce:para: Using CDs, gold, and Naphthalimide, a sensor assembly for recognition of Cys have been designed and synthesized. For the preparation of designed sensor assembly, CDs and naphthalimide derivative (L1) were synthesized separately and then combined in the form a ratiometric sensor assembly using a gold nanoparticle as a linker unit between CDs and L1 as shown in scheme 1 . CDs were synthesized using citric acid and ethylenediamine (EDA) by hydrothermal method [46]. A mixture of citric acid and EDA was heated at 180 °C in Teflon lined autoclave for 6 h and after cooling to room temperature, dots were purified through dialysis membrane (MW 3500 D) against water for 48 h. Purified CDs were characterized using different analytical tools. Spherical morphology and excellent uniform dispersion of dots were observed from TEM image with particle size about 4 nm (Fig. S1). The hydrodynamic diameter of the particle (9 nm) analyzed from the histogram of DLS (Fig. S2 A). The relatively higher diameter than particle size as calculated from TEM analysis suggested the interaction of solvent molecule around the particles. Broad diffraction peak centered at 19° (2θ) in powder XRD advised the disorder of carbon atom on CDs surface (Fig. 1 A). Furthermore, the presence of functional groups on CDs surface were analyzed with FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1B). The broad absorption band at 3400 – 3100 cm−1 represent the −OH and –NH stretches. Sharp band at 1675 cm-1 characterize the −CONH stretches. Small band at 1390 cm−1 represent the C–N stretches while alky chain deformation corresponding to the 1340 cm−1 band [47].

Photophysical properties of CDs have been assessed by recording the UV–vis absorption and emission spectra. CDs has exhibited two absorption bands at 335 and 248 nm corresponding to different transitions states on CDs surface in aqueous solution (Fig. 1 C) [48]. Under the UV light (365 nm), an aqueous solution of CDs has bright blue emission suggested the strong fluorescence emission of CDs and light brown in day-light. A typically excitation-dependent emission is a characteristic feature of CDs depicted from the fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1D). The decrease in fluorescence intensity along with a redshift in emission maxima as the excitation wavelength was increased from 340 nm to 500 nm was clearly observed from the emission spectrum. Maximum emission intensity (ϕ = 23%) was observed at 445 nm when CDs were excited at 340 nm wavelength. To explain the emission nature of CDs, various research groups, have put forward the theories like surface edge model, heteroatom model, quantum confinement, surface trap model in last decades, however, the exact mechanism is still uncertain [49,50]. Furthermore, the stability of fluorescence emission of CDs at different pH and salt concentration was evaluated. pH from 4 to 8 ha s not produced any remarkable influence over fluorescence emission intensity of CDs at λem 445 nm and CDs has also retained their fluorescence in the presence of salt concentration up to 2 mM (Fig. S3A, S3B). All these results demonstrate that CDs are stable at a wide range of pH and salt concentrations, which makes CDs excellent candidates for bioimaging in complex living tissues. The energy acceptor molecule L1 was synthesized in multi-step reaction as shown in scheme 1 . 3-aminonaphthalic anhydride was synthesized by nitration of 1, 8-naphthalic anhydride followed by reduction of the nitro group with SnCl2 in HCl. 3-aminonaphthalic anhydride was further treated with cystamine to get the final ligand L1. Synthesized ligand L1 was characterized with NMR and mass spectroscopy (Fig. S8-S11).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of FRET pair and cys sensing mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To construct the FRET mechanism between two fluorophores, both energy acceptor, and energy donor units must be at a particular distance from each other and fluorescence spectra of one fluorophore must be significantly overlapped with absorption spectra of another fluorophore unit [51–53]. If any two fluorophores full fill these conditions, they could form the FRET mechanism. As we observed, emission spectra of CDs were significantly overlapped with the absorption spectra of L1, which suggested that the energy of CDs can be transferred from CDs to L1 (Fig. S4). To transfer the energy of CDs to L1, we have chosen the gold nanoparticles as a solid support so that the two unit come close to each other for efficient transfer of energy from CDs to L1. Sensor assembly was prepared by in situ reduction of gold salt to gold nanoparticles in the presence of CDs and L1 at room temperature. Herein, in situ synthesized gold nanoparticle prevailed dual functions: (1) Act as a linker between two fluorescent molecules and (2) A substrate for Cys molecule. Both CDs and L1 units assembled on gold nanoparticles surface through Au-N and Au-S interactions respectively. Disulfide bond of L1 reduced to thiol and interacted with gold nanoparticle through Au-S interactions. Prepared sensor assembly was characterized by UV–vis absorption, Fluorescence spectroscopy, and TEM images. UV–vis absorption spectra of sensor assembly exhibited three different absorption band at 346 nm, 440 nm and 551 nm which are correspondence to the CDs, naphthalimide and gold moieties respectively (Fig. 2 A). Therefore, these absorption band suggested the presence of all these molecules in the sensor assembly. Furthermore, we observed that when sensor assembly was excited at 365 nm wavelength, emission at 568 nm emerged which signifies the emission intensity of L1 which is only possible if CDs and L1 are interconnected to each other. Morphology of assembly was recorded from TEM images. TEM images reveal that gold nanoparticles in the assembly were in a cluster form with fair spherical morphology. Adsorbed CDs over gold nanoparticles surface were clearly observed from TEM images as shown in Fig. 2B. Increase in hydrodynamic particle diameter of after synthesis of assembly was found to be 20 nm (Fig. S2 A). The emission aroused from the Sensor assembly at 568 nm when excited at 340 nm also confirmed the transfer of energy from CDs to naphthalimide through gold linkage and formation of an interconnected link between CDs and naph moiety. Transfer of energy from CDs to naph moiety is termed as FRET mechanism. The FRET efficiency of assembly was calculated by following the equation: Fa/Fd + Fa (Fluorescence emission intensity of acceptor unit and Fd is the Fluorescence emission intensity of donor unit). In the absence of Cys, it was calculated to be 1.35. Furthermore, the stability of FRET pair system was evaluated at different pH and salt concentrations. Fluorescence spectra of Sensor assembly was recorded by varying the pH from 3 to 10 and it was found that pH 5–8 is the most appropriate range for sensing of Cys in which no significant change in fluorescence spectra was observed. Stable fluorescence emission of sensor assembly at different pH suggested that sensor complex could be stable in the microenvironment of cells where the variation in pH is commonly associated with a various disease state (Fig. S5A). Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of assembly was also stable up to 1 mM concentration of NaCl (Fig. S5B). For the sensing study of Cys, 20 μg/mL solution of Sensor assembly was prepared in PBS and change in fluorescence signal at two different wavelengths, 568 nm and 450 nm with the progressive addition of Cys from 0 to 200 μM was recorded. Due to the high affinity of –SH group towards gold nanoparticle surface, Cys molecules displaced the CDs from Au surface which leads to the elimination of FRET mechanism. Due to FRET elimination, fluorescence emission at 568 nm gradually starts to decrease while emission at 450 nm was enhanced with the progressive addition of Cys (Fig. 2C). The change in emission intensity at 568 and 450 nm shows a strong linear fit (R2 = 0.987) with the increased concentration of Cys (Fig. 2D). The limit of detection (5 nM) for Cys was calculated by the equation: LOD = 3 × SD/k (SD: standard deviation of blank sample, k: slope = 0.022) which is comparable to most of the reported probes (Table S1). Elimination of energy transfer from CDs to L1 was also confirmed by recording the fluorescence decay time. Increase in decay time of Sensor assembly from τav 3 ns to τav 5 ns which is approximate to the plain CDs (6.7 ns) demonstrate the displacement CDs from the gold nanoparticle surface (Fig. S6). Increase in quantum yield from 9% to 19% of the CDs after addition of Cys also suggested the release of CDs from complex and elimination of FRET mechanism. Thus, the working principle of Sensor assembly for Cys sensing has been predicted and outlined which is based on the displacement of CDs from Au nanoparticle surface with the addition of Cys (Fig. 2C, F).

The selectivity of sensor complex towards Cys was determined by recording the fluorescence emission spectra of sensor complex in the presence of different amino acids and thiol-bearing biomolecules such as glutathione and homocysteine under similar conditions. Although, the intracellular concentration of Glu is higher than the Cys and structurally similar to Cys, makes hurdles to specifically sensing of Cys in living cells. However, as shown in Fig. 2E, molecules (Ala, Gly, Ile, Hcy, Met, Asp, Ile, GSH at 2 μM and H2O2 at 100 nM) did not produce any significant changes in fluorescence emission of the sensor assembly in contrast to Cys. These results also suggested the high selectivity of sensor complex towards Cys molecules over the Glu, which is the main interference component for Cys sensing. On addition of high concentration of hydrogen peroxide (100 nM) leads to decomposition of gold nanoparticles which may disassemble the sensor assembly. As the gold nanoparticle decompose, both CDs and L3 go far apart from each other and thus elimination of FRET pair between two fluorophores. This small positive response by hydrogen peroxide observed only at high concentration. Therefore, sensor assembly could be used for the determination of Cys in blood plasma as well as in cytoplasm of cells with negligible suffering from the interference of related biomolecules (Table 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of sensor complex for blood serum and cellular imaging

ce:section.ce:para: Potential of cytotoxicity of CDs and sensor assembly at different concentration towards HeLa cells was determined by performing MTT assay. More than 90% of cells remained viable after treatment with CDs (100 μg/mL) suggested the negligible toxicity of CDs towards mammalian cells. Furthermore, sensor assembly was evaluated for cytotoxicity assay and found that assembly did not produce any apparent toxicity to mammalian cells up to 50 μg/mL concentrations, however at 100 μg/mL cell viability reduced to 84% (Fig. S7). These results indicate that sensor complex is less cytotoxic at 50 μg/mL concentration, however, exhibit little cytotoxicity at higher concentration. Therefore, we could say that prepared sensor assembly is the best suitable approach for the sensing of endogenous Cys with acceptable cytotoxicity at a higher dose.

In conclusion, a ratiometric model having high selectivity for Cys sensing has been developed. Assembly was designed by hybridization of CDs, gold and organic fluorophore L1 to eliminate the drawbacks of single emission sensor. Upon addition of Cys to sensor solution, CDs are displaced from gold nanoparticles surface and the FRET mechanism between the CDs and L1 has been eliminated. Importantly, sensor complex is easily penetrate inside the cells and provides reliable emission in the cytoplasm of cells in response to Cys. Change in the color emission from yellow to blue clearly observed under the confocal laser scanning microscope. Biocompatibility of sensor was evaluated by performing MTT assay against HeLa cell lines. Therefore, our strategies may provide new insight into the development of a ratiometric sensor for Cys determination and cellular bioimaging which may help to the diagnosis of diseases associated with Cys level in living tissues.

1, 8-naphthalic anhydride, cysteine, glutathione, and gold were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Citric acid was obtained from Avra Synthesis. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), DMEM and DMSO supplied by Hi-Media. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

1HNMR (400) MHz and 13CNMR (100) MHz were recorded on Jeol instruments. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded on Shimadzu UV-2400 and Perkin Elmer LS 55 fluorescence spectrophotometer respectively. TEM images were recorded using Hitachi (H-7500) instrument. Particle diameter in solution phase was analyzed from DLS histogram of Metrohm Microtrac Ultra Nanotrac particle size analyzer. IR spectrum of solid samples were recorded by using solid cell technique on Bruker Tensor 27 spectrophotometer.

CDs were prepared using the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 500 mg of citric acid and 1 mL of ethylenediamine were added into the 20 mL of distilled water and the mixture was transferred to Teflon lined autoclave. The autoclave was placed into a furnace preheated at 180 °C for 6 h. Let the autoclave to cool at room temperature and reaction mixture was dialyzed against water for 48 h to remove unreacted organic materials. CDs were freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for further use.

S-Nitro and S-amino were synthesized according to literature reports. [46] 3-aminonaphthalic anhydride (213 mg, 1 mmol) and cystamine dihydrochloride (112.5 mg, 0.5 mmol) were dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol. 0.5 mmol of triethylamine was added to the mixture and refluxed for 4 h. Yellow precipitates were filtered, washed two times with ethanol and dried under vacuum to get desired product L1 as a yellow solid. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6) δ 8.01 – 7.97 (m, 4 H), 7.91 (s, 2 H), 7.57 – 7.53 (m, 2 H), 7.22 (s, 2 H), 5.96 (s, 4 H), 4.32 – 4.29 (m, 4 H), 3.02 – 2.98 (m, 4 H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO-D 6) δ 164.20, 164.04, 148.37, 134.04, 132.08, 127.42, 126.00, 122.97, 122.28, 122.16, 121.11, 112.29, 39.51, 35.63.

To 5 mL of 20 μg/mL solution of CDs, 1 mL of aqueous solution of gold (0.01 M) was added and stirred the reaction mixture for 30 min. 1 mL of 0.1 mg/mL solution of L1 in DMSO was slowly added to the reaction mixture over the 1 h and let the reaction mixture to stirred for 48 h in the absence of light. Centrifuged the solution at 1000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large particles and dialyzed against water for 48 h. The final product was freeze-dried and stored in dark condition.

0.1 mM stock solution of cysteine was incubated in NaOH for 2 h (pH 10) and diluted to the appropriate concentration for recording the fluorescence spectra of sensor assembly in PBS. Sensor solution (20 μg/ml) was also prepared in PBS and titration with successive addition of Cys solution carried out at 37 °C. Sensor solution was incubated for 10 min after each addition. Similar conditions followed for the analyte.

The concentration of 0, 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 μM solution of cysteine added into the 5% blood serum and incubated for 2 h at pH 12. Prepared solution was inoculated with 20 μg/mL solution of sensor assembly and further incubated for 10 min. Fluorescence spectra was recorded at 37 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular imaging of cys in HeLa cells
ce:section.ce:para: For the imaging of Cys in the cytoplasm of cells, HeLa cells were incubated in six-well plates at 37 °C in the presence of 5% CO2. After 24 h of incubation of cells, old media was exchanged with new media containing sensor complex 50 μg/mL and incubated for 1 h. Cell culture was washed three times with PBS to remove the extracellular sensor complex and further incubated for 2 h. Images of cells were collected under a fluorescence microscope after 2 h of incubation. For the negative control, cells were treated with NMM for 1 h before incubation with sensor complex. After 1 h of treatment with NMM, cell culture was washed with PBS and further treated with sensor complex for 1 h.

HeLa cells were grown in DMEM media containing 10% FBS in 96 well plate for 24 h. After successful washing with PBS, cells were treated with different concentration of CDs and sensor assembly in triplicates. MTT solution was added to the cultured cells and absorbance of cells was measured using microplate reader after lysis with DMSO. Each result of cells viability represents the average of triplicate experiments. Error bars represent the standard deviation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All authors are very thankful to DST-SERB (EMR/2014/000613) for providing financial sport to complete the scientific work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.105.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of l-Cysteine and transition metal ions on dimethyl sulfide oxidation
Preferred hydrogen-bonding partners of cysteine: implications for regulating cys functions
BODIPY-based fluorometric sensor for the simultaneous determination of cys, hcy, and GSH in human serum
A cysteine sensor based on a gold nanoparticle-iron phthalocyanine modified graphite paste electrode
High-throughput endogenous measurement of S-nitrosylation in Alzheimer’s disease using oxidized cysteine-selective cPILOT
Diverse functional roles of reactive cysteines
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of cysteine and homocysteine with a BODIPY dye by mimicking the native chemical ligation
A highly selective fluorescence turn-on sensor for cysteine/homocysteine and its application in bioimaging
One-step synthesis of CuO–Cu2O heterojunction by flame spray pyrolysis for cathodic photoelectrochemical sensing of l-Cysteine
Fluorescent sensors for measuring metal ions in living systems
Fluorescence “Turn-On” Indicator displacement assay-based sensing of nitroexplosive 2,4,6-Trinitrophenol in aqueous media via a polyelectrolyte and dye complex
A diarylethene-based "on-off-on" fluorescence sensor for the sequential recognition of mercury and cysteine
Assembling of a functional cyclodextrin-decorated upconversion luminescence nanoplatform for cysteine-sensing
Yolk–shell upconversion nanocomposites for LRET sensing of cysteine/homocysteine
Flavone-based ESIPT ratiometric chemodosimeter for detection of cysteine in living cells
Highly selective colorimetric and fluorescent BODIPY dyes for sensing of cysteine and/or homocysteine
Nanosensor composed of nitrogen-doped carbon dots and gold nanoparticles for highly selective detection of cysteine with multiple signals
N-doped graphene quantum dots-decorated V2O5 nanosheet for fluorescence turn off–On detection of cysteine
Luminescence properties and energy transfer in Tb3+ and Eu3+ co-doped Ba2P2O7 phosphors
A general strategy for the semisynthesis of ratiometric fluorescent sensor proteins with increased dynamic range
Molecular imprinting ratiometric fluorescence sensor for highly selective and sensitive detection of phycocyanin
Cell permeable ratiometric fluorescent sensors for imaging phosphoinositides
Ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on inhibition of resonance for detection of cadmium in aqueous solution and living cells
Characteristic excitation wavelength dependence of fluorescence emissions in carbon “Quantum” dots
Ultrasensitive optical detection of anions by quantum dots
Rangefinder: a semisynthetic FRET sensor design algorithm
Critical comparison of FRET-Sensor functionality in the cytosol and endoplasmic reticulum and implications for quantification of ions
A genetically encoded FRET sensor for intracellular heme
High-capacitance hybrid supercapacitor based on multi-colored fluorescent carbon-dots
Nitric oxide sensing through azo-dye formation on carbon dots
Carbon dots based photoelectrochemical sensors for ultrasensitive detection of glutathione and its applications in probing of myocardial infarction
Carbon dots as photosensitisers for solar-driven catalysis
Nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, highly photoluminescent carbon dots from ammonium citrate
Highly selective fluorogenic multianalyte biosensors constructed via enzyme-catalyzed coupling and aggregation-induced emission
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging
A highly sensitive and selective fluorimetric probe for intracellular peroxynitrite based on photoinduced electron transfer from ferrocene to carbon dots
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
Super-resolution fluorescence imaging of biocompatible carbon dots
Green synthesis of luminescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from milk and its imaging application
Histidine-derived nontoxic nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensing and bioimaging applications
Self-assembly of monodisperse carbon dots into high-brightness nanoaggregates for cellular uptake imaging and iron(III) sensing
Computational approaches to cell-nanomaterial interactions: keeping balance between therapeutic efficiency and cytotoxicity
Carbon dots based FRET for the detection of DNA damage
Amino nitrogen quantum dots-based nanoprobe for fluorescence detection and imaging of cysteine in biological samples
Carbon dot based, naphthalimide coupled FRET pair for highly selective ratiometric detection of thioredoxin reductase and cancer screening
Molecular fluorescence in citric acid-based carbon dots
Carbon dots with continuously tunable full-color emission and their application in ratiometric pH sensing
Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-Controlled luminescence mechanism
Activating room temperature long afterglow of carbon dots via covalent fixation
Design consideration and probes for fluorescence resonance energy transfer studies
FRET-based small-molecule fluorescent probes: rational design and bioimaging applications
Fluorescent protein based FRET pairs with improved dynamic range for fluorescence lifetime measurements
Construction of an upconversion nanoprobe with Few-Atom Silver Nanoclusters as the energy acceptor

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jagpreet Singh Sidhu: He is currently a PhD student in a department of Chemistry at Indian Institute of Technology, Ropar. He did his master in Pharmacy in Medicinal chemistry from Central University of Punjab, Bathinda. His research work focus on the development of fluorescent probe for biomolecules/enzymes recognition and cellular Imaging.

Ashutosh Singh: received his M.Sc. degree in Biotechnology from Hemwati Nandan Bahuguna University, Srinagar, Uttarakhand, India in 2011. He is currently pursuing Ph.D. in Biology from School of Basic Sciences, IIT Mandi, India. His research interest includes the regulation of microRNAs by oncogenic transcriptional factors in Medulloblastoma cancer stem cells. Secondarily, he is also working on different aspects of diagnosis and therapeutics of cancer.

Navneet Kaur: received her Ph.D. in 2005 from National Institute of Pharmaceutical Education and Research (NIPER), Mohali, India. Currently, she is an associate professor in Department of Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, India. Her research interests include medicinal chemistry, organic nanomaterial development and evaluation of their recognition behaviour. She has published more than 100 research papers in reputed international journals.

Narinder Singh: Professor Narinder Singh completed his Ph.D. from Guru Nanak Dev University, Amritsar. Currently he is working as an Associate Professor at the Department ofChemistry, IIT Ropar. He has published more than 175 research papers in inter-national peer-reviewed journals. His areas of research include supramolecular andmaterials chemistry for the application of chemo- and bio-sensors, photo-detectors,and the synthesis of new organic receptors.
